summary: Nine concrete, comment-endorsed tools, experiences, and learning resources from the current Hacker News front page.
telegram_footer: 9 items surfaced | 30/30 current front-page stories processed | history + run artifact updated.

items.name: Jelly UI
items.source_story_title: Jelly UI: Soft-body physics for native HTML form controls
items.summary: A web-component library that adds soft-body physics to HTML form controls.
items.why_included: Several commenters independently praised its creative, usable interaction design.
items.evidence: One commenter called it “stupidly creative” and said it felt naturally intuitive; another praised its web-component implementation.

items.name: GNU Parallel
items.source_story_title: I wrote an bash enumerator because I was sick of xargs
items.summary: A command-line utility for running shell jobs concurrently with rich controls.
items.why_included: It received specific first-hand praise for making batch work safer and more approachable.
items.evidence: A user said GNU Parallel’s `--dry-run` made them confident enough to do more batch processing than with xargs.

items.name: Paseo
items.summary: A desktop and mobile interface for running local coding-agent CLIs.
items.why_included: A direct recommendation identified it as a strong companion interface for existing agent CLIs.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended paseo.sh as “a great interface for desktop and mobile” with Claude, OpenCode, or Pi running underneath.

items.name: Pengy
items.summary: An open agent specification and reference project for building custom tool-using agents.
items.why_included: It was recommended as a practical, inspectable starting point for a tailored agent system.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended Pengy’s `spec.md` as a blueprint to feed to an agent and adapt to one’s own stack and workflow.

items.name: Immersive Gaussian Splat Tour of Grace Cathedral
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Immersive Gaussian Splat tour of grace cathedral, San Francisco
items.summary: A mobile-friendly interactive 3D Gaussian-splat tour of San Francisco’s Grace Cathedral.
items.why_included: The tour earned unusually strong praise for its visual quality and mobile experience.
items.evidence: A commenter called the tour “such a cool immersive experience on mobile,” while another described the stained glass and cutaway view as stunning.

items.name: Bloomy
items.source_story_title: Launch HN: Bloomy (YC S26) – AI-powered mastery learning for K-12
items.summary: An AI-assisted K-12 mastery-learning platform with adaptive lessons and Socratic tutoring.
items.why_included: It received direct enthusiasm from educators alongside substantive discussion of learning design.
items.evidence: One commenter said “HELL YES and TAKE MY MONEY”; a former teacher said a program like Bloomy could have enabled better student relationships.

items.name: The Psychology of Computer Programming
items.summary: Gerald Weinberg’s classic book on the human and social side of programming.
items.why_included: It was singled out as a standout resource for understanding software-team psychology.
items.evidence: A commenter described Weinberg’s book as “one of the best pieces of work in this genre.”

items.name: Shinjuku Station in 3D
items.summary: An interactive 3D visualization of Tokyo’s vast Shinjuku Station complex.
items.why_included: The visualizer was praised as both excellent and potentially useful for real-world navigation training.
items.evidence: A commenter called it “an excellent visualization” and proposed turning it into a first-person navigation trainer for visitors.

items.name: The Exit 8
items.summary: A short anomaly-spotting walking simulator set in an endless Japanese underground passage.
items.why_included: A firsthand player recommendation surfaced it as an enjoyable, compact liminal-space game.
items.evidence: A commenter said they expected a Shinjuku navigation game, but found that The Exit 8’s single-hallway premise was “still a lot of fun.”
